# Technical Memorandum

Date: December 23, 2021

To: Dean Wright, PG, Project Manager

California Department of Toxic Substances Control

From: John E. Juhrend, PE, CEG, Project Manager

Geocon Consultants, Inc.

Subject: Draft Interim Removal Action Completion Report

Caltrans Modesto Soil Stockpiles

Phase 1 Interim State Route 132 West Project, Modesto, California

This Technical Memorandum has been prepared to present a progress summary of barium-containing soil (BCS) grading and clean fill capping operations completed to date for the Caltrans Modesto Soil Stockpiles located within Interim Phase I of the State Route 132 West Project in Modesto, California. The California Department of Toxic Substances Control (DTSC) is the lead regulatory agency providing site mitigation oversight under a Caltrans Interagency Agreement.

The attached incomplete Draft Interim Removal Action Completion Report (RACR) summarizes the current status of removal, consolidation and clean fill capping of BCS Stockpiles as highway embankment fill during Phase I construction as specified in the 2019 Remedial Design Implementation Plan. The BCS Stockpiles and aerially deposited lead impacted soil removed from the unpaved shoulder of northbound State Route 99 have been consolidated and capped with DTSC-approved clean fill materials. Remaining work includes additional placement of structural pavement section on the upper surface of the capped BCS Stockpiles, erosion control, drainage, and median barrier and signage.

A Final Interim RACR will be submitted following completed construction of Interim Phase I of the SR 132 West Project scheduled for opening to traffic in September 2022.

Mr. Wright:

In accordance with the Interagency Agreement between the California Department of Toxic Substances Control and the California Department of Transportation (Caltrans), we are pleased to submit this Interim Removal Action Completion Report (RACR) for the Caltrans Modesto Soil Stockpiles (the Site). The Site is located along the eastern portion of the alignment of the newly constructed State Route (SR) 132 West Project in Modesto, Stanislaus County, California.

This Interim RACR has been prepared to document the status of consolidation and clean fill capping of barium-containing soil as highway embankment fill during Phase I construction of the SR 132 West Project as specified in the referenced Remedial Design Implementation Plan. A Final RACR will be submitted following Ultimate Phase 2 construction of the SR 132 West Project scheduled for completion in 2028.

Please contact us should you have any questions regarding this Interim RACR.

#### INTERIM REMOVAL ACTION COMPLETION REPORT CALTRANS MODESTO SOIL STOCKPILES SR 132 WEST PROJECT - INTERIM PHASE 1

#### 1.0 INTRODUCTION

This Interim Removal Action Completion Report (RACR) has been prepared by Geocon Consultants, Inc. on behalf of the City of Modesto (City) and the California Department of Transportation (Caltrans) for the Caltrans Modesto Soil Stockpiles (the Site). The Site is located along the eastern portion of the alignment of the newly constructed State Route (SR) 132 West Project in Modesto, Stanislaus County, California (Figure 1). The California Department of Toxic Substances Control (DTSC), in cooperation with the Central Valley Regional Water Quality Control Board (CVRWQCB), is the lead regulatory agency providing site mitigation oversight under a Caltrans Interagency Agreement.

This RACR has been prepared to document the status of consolidation and clean fill capping of bariumcontaining soil (BCS) Stockpiles 1 through 3 as highway embankment fill during Interim Phase 1 construction of the SR 132 West Project in accordance with the 2019 Remedial Design Implementation Plan (RDIP, Geocon, 2019a) and Caltrans-approved project design plans and specifications. The RDIP provided the technical approach and detailed plans for interim implementation of the selected remedial remedy identified in the Final Remedial Action Plan (RAP, Geocon, 2018). The selected remedial alternative to mitigate exposure to BCS was consolidation and containment by incorporating the stockpiles as highway embankment fill and covering with clean fill and structural pavement section during Interim Phase 1 and Ultimate Phase 2 construction of the SR 132 West Project. A Final RACR will be submitted following Ultimate Phase 2 construction of the SR 132 West Project scheduled for completion in 2028.

The SR 132 West Project will result in the ultimate build-out of a four-lane expressway. Interim Phase 1 of the project includes the construction of two of four planned traffic lanes from east of SR 99 to North Dakota Avenue and related highway bridge structures and interchanges. BCS Stockpiles 1 through 3, portions of which contain elevated levels of barium and lead, have been contained during Interim Phase 1 of the project by utilizing them as clean capped embankment material within the overall limits of BCS Stockpiles 1 and 2 located westerly of SR 99. Ultimate Phase 2 construction will include highway widening to four traffic lanes.

#### 1.1 Site Description

Prior to construction of Interim Phase 1 of the SR 132 West Project, the Site consisted of BCS Stockpiles 1 through 3 within Caltrans SR 132 right-of-way (ROW) as depicted on the Preconstruction Site Plan, Figure 2. The BCS Stockpiles are primarily comprised of layered, poorly graded sand and silty sand similar to underlying native alluvial deposits of the Modesto Formation. The average maximum stockpile fill thickness was approximately 20 feet.

BCS Stockpile 1, located approximately 150 feet westerly of Emerald Avenue, was roughly 600 feet long in the east-west direction, 160 feet wide, and had an estimated fill volume of 34,000 cubic yards (yd<sup>3</sup> ). BCS Stockpile 2, located between Emerald Avenue and SR 99, was roughly 1,650 feet long in the east-west direction, 160 feet wide, and had an estimated fill volume of 102,000 yd<sup>3</sup> . BCS Stockpile 3, formerly located on the east side of SR 99, was roughly 1,100 feet long, 120 feet wide, and had an estimated fill volume of 24,000 yd<sup>3</sup> . BCS Stockpile 3 was completely excavated and placed as highway embankment fill on top of Stockpile 1 and between Stockpile 1 and Emerald Avenue.

Prior to commencing the highway construction activities, ten groundwater monitoring wells (MW-1 through MW-10) located adjacent and nearby to the BCS Stockpiles where periodically sampled. Depth to groundwater measured in October 2021 ranged between approximately 39 to 43 feet (below natural grade) with historic flow generally toward the south and southeast (Stantec, 2021). Due to conflicts with planned highway improvements, six of the ten monitoring wells were destroyed in April 2019 (Geocon, 2019b). Groundwater extraction/dewatering was not required during construction of Interim Phase 1 of the SR 132 West Project in the immediate vicinity of the BCS Stockpiles.

The pre-construction BCS Stockpile locations, Caltrans SR 132 ROW boundaries, and former and existing groundwater monitoring well locations are depicted on Figure 2.

#### 1.2 Site History and Environmental Investigations

From the 1930s to 1970s, property beneath and northeast of the SR 99/Kansas Avenue Interchange was occupied by chemical processing facilities operated by Food Machinery and Chemical Corporation (FMC) and its predecessors. Ores and minerals including barite (barium sulfate) and celestite (strontium sulfate) were processed for use in greases, lubricating oil and pigment blanks. Sodium sulfide was generated as a by-product and sold as a caustic and reagent. From the 1950s to the 1970s, liquid residue ("tailings") generated at this facility was discharged to unlined evaporation ponds. In 1961, the State of California purchased a 4.3-acre parcel in the southwestern portion of the FMC facility, including a portion of the ponds, for the construction of the SR 99 freeway through Modesto. Pond tailings and underlying soils from the FMC property along with native soils excavated south of the SR 99/Kansas Avenue interchange were subsequently placed to create BCS Stockpiles 1 through 3.

An Initial Site Assessment completed for Caltrans for the SR 132 West Project in 2003 identified a potential for the soil stockpiles to contain residual chemicals associated with the former FMC impoundments. A Preliminary Site Investigation completed in 2004 and subsequent site characterization studies performed for Caltrans confirmed the presence of elevated barium concentrations (and to a lesser extent lead) in stockpile soil samples exceeding regulatory healthbased screening levels. The results of groundwater monitoring activities at the Site indicated that the identified contaminants were reported at concentrations less than California Maximum Contaminant Levels for drinking water. The results of a 2007 Human Health Risk Assessment

(HHRA) and 2013 HHRA Update prepared for Caltrans indicated that the soil stockpiles did not pose an unacceptable risk or hazard to current or future offsite residents, trespassers, construction workers or hypothetical future shallow groundwater users.

In a letter dated December 17, 2009, the DTSC stated that:

"DTSC finds that the soil stockpiles, as currently managed by Caltrans on Caltrans property, do not pose a risk to human health for: 1) Caltrans workers who access the fenced site to conduct mowing operations, conduct fence repairs, or other routine activities; 2) trespassers; and 3) residents adjacent to the stockpiles. Until such time that the State Route 132/99 Interchange project is constructed and/or the final disposition of the soil stockpiles is determined, Caltrans should continue to manage the soil stockpiles by: 1) limiting access to Caltrans authorized personnel; 2) inspecting and maintaining the chain-link fence; 3) prohibiting any activities involving excavation/grading, off-site removal of soil, or placement of other soil on the Site; and 4) maintaining the current grade and vegetative cover. Caltrans should also maintain the existing groundwater monitoring system associated with the Site."

Prior to recent stockpile grading activities associated with construction of Interim Phase 1 of the SR 132 West Project, the only previous grading/removal action completed at the Site was excavation and landfill disposal of approximately 2,800 yd<sup>3</sup> of the northern portion of Stockpile 3 in 2012 under DTSC oversight as part of Caltrans' rehabilitation of the SR 99 northbound off-ramp to Kansas Avenue to improve traffic safety and meet current design standards. The highway safety improvement project included widening the off-ramp shoulder areas and associated drainage features. Shoulder widening on the east side of the offramp included construction of a retaining wall following partial removal of the Stockpile 3 embankment slope. The results of air monitoring completed during the BCS Stockpile 3 remedial excavation activities indicated that airborne levels of barium and lead were well below regulatory thresholds.

A Feasibility Study (FS, Geocon, 2014) and RAP (Geocon, 2018) were prepared for the Site in 2014 and 2018, respectively, and subsequently approved by DTSC. The FS evaluated four remedial alternatives including No Action, Institutional Controls, Removal, and Containment. Based on the alternative evaluation criteria, the DTSC-approved long-term remedial measure to mitigate potential exposure to the BCS Stockpiles was "Alternative 4 - Containment" to be implemented during Interim Phase 1 and Ultimate Phase 2 construction of the SR 132 West Project. As outlined in the RAP, containment of the BCS Stockpiles was achieved by utilization/consolidation of the soil stockpile material generally within the existing stockpile footprint as highway embankment fills, and covering with Caltrans-maintained structural pavement section and/or clean fill material.

# 1.3 Remediation Plan

The 2019 RDIP was prepared to present the remedial design, BCS management procedures, and construction monitoring and regulatory reporting protocols to be implemented during interim containment (encapsulation) of the soil stockpile material in general accordance with the criteria and requirements presented in the Final RAP. The RDIP was specific to soil stockpile containment measures implemented during Interim Phase 1 construction of the SR 132 West Project. A supplemental RDIP will be prepared for the Ultimate Phase 2 portion of the project.

The RDIP provided specific design details regarding the containment of the three BCS Stockpiles within SR 132 highway embankment fills (BCS Containment Zones) located between SR 99 and the west end of Stockpile 1. Excavated BCS for placement within the BCS Containment Zones was generated from removal of Stockpile 3, bridge abutment excavations at either end of Stockpile 2, and mechanically stabilized earth (MSE) retaining wall foundation excavations along the southern boundaries of Stockpiles 1 and 2. The RDIP further provided for the placement of shallow soil excavated along the northbound SR 99 shoulder that contained hazardous waste concentrations of aerially deposited lead (ADL) to be placed within the BCS Containment Zones.

The RDIP provided the following soil excavation verification thresholds for the planned BCS removal areas:

Barium<sup>1</sup> - 1,000 milligrams per kilogram (mg/kg) based on groundwater protection

Lead - 80 mg/kg based on DTSC residential screening level

<sup>1</sup>Note: Regulatory residential Environmental Screening Level (ESL) for barium is 3,000 mg/kg

The RDIP further provided for sampling and analytical testing of planned clean fill source areas identified within the SR 132 West Project boundaries in accordance with DTSC's clean fill material guidance (DTSC, 2001). A DTSC-approved Variance to the RDIP dated April 9, 2020 was prepared by Geocon to identify planned clean fill source borrow areas within the project boundaries including MSE retaining wall foundation excavations adjacent to the southerly boundaries of BCS Stockpiles 1 and 2. The Variance further presented the following site-specific upperbound background concentrations for barium and lead for comparison with proposed clean fill source area characterization soil samples:

 Barium - 120 mg/kg Lead - 3.8 mg/kg

A second DTSC-approved Variance to the RDIP dated September 2, 2020 was prepared by Geocon to provide clarification of the alternative use of a 4-inch-thick layer of Class 3 aggregate base (AB) over the top of the completed BCS Containment Zones versus a 4-inch-thick layer of AB as specified in the RDIP. Copies of the DTSC-approved RDIP Variances are in Appendix A.

# 1.4 Report Organization

This Interim RACR contains the following sections and contents:

- Section 1.0 Introduction provides a description of the Site, brief overview of site history and environmental investigations, prior removal action, and purpose of the RACR.
- Section 2.0 Project Organization describes the project team organizations, and roles and responsibilities with respect to implementing the RDIP.
- Section 3.0 RDIP Activities and BCS Containment Implementation presents a detailed description of the completed RDIP and BCS Stockpile containment and clean capping activities.
- Section 4.0 RDIP Monitoring summarizes the environmental and health and safety site monitoring as outlined in the RDIP
- Section 5.0 Post Construction Monitoring/Maintenance describes monitoring and maintenance activities to be performed to ensure that the implemented BCS containment measures continue to be effective.
- Section 6.0 Project Websites and References provides a list of websites and references cited.

# 2.0 PROJECT ORGANIZATION

This section describes the role and responsibilities of the following project team organizations responsible for implementation of the RDIP during Interim Phase 1 construction of the SR 132 West Project:

Site Owner/Responsible Party: State of California/Caltrans

Regulatory Agencies: DTSC and CVRWQCB SR 132 West Project Proponent: City of Modesto

Project Proponent's Civil Engineer: Dokken Engineering

Project Proponent's Construction Manager: WSP

Project Proponent's Construction Contractor: Bay Cities Paving & Grading (BCPG)

Project Proponent's Environmental Consultant: Geocon Consultants, Inc. Project Proponent's Geotechnical Testing and Inspection: Kleinfelder Inc.

#### 2.1 Project Team Responsibilities

Caltrans is responsible for the implementation of the RDIP and future monitoring and maintenance of the BCS Containment Zone areas of the SR 132 West Project under regulatory oversight and approval by DTSC in cooperation with the CVRWQCB. Caltrans is further responsible for ongoing groundwater and strormwater monitoring activities specific to the BCS Stockpiles.

Caltrans, DTSC and the City are responsible for public outreach and participation including the following completed activities:

- Community Open House Meeting prior to construction (November 19, 2019);
- Public distribution of DTSC-prepared Start Work Notice;
- Electronic posting of periodic project updates.

The City is responsible for Interim Phase 1 and Ultimate Phase 2 project design and construction. The City further coordinated initial clearing of the Site including tree and stump removal, cleanup of homeless encampments, and installation of time-lapse video cameras in the vicinity of the BCS Stockpiles. Dokken, under contract with the City, provided final design, construction specifications and design support for Interim Phase 1 of the SR 132 West Project.

The City's construction management firm (WSP) was responsible for oversight of the highway construction contractor (BCPG) including review and approval of contactor submittals, daily field inspection, and completed work compliance verification with project specifications and the RDIP. WSP's geotechnical consultant (Kleinfedler) was responsible for insitu testing of placed BCS and ADL soil within the Stockpile 1 and 2 BCS Containment Zones to verify proper compaction in accordance with the project specifications.

WSP and BCPG were responsible for executing the following work associated with the management of BCS Stockpiles with oversight coordination provided by Caltrans and Geocon:

- Worker Health and Safety Plan;
- Excavation and Transportation Plan;
- Onsite worker BCS Stockpile awareness training;
- Mobilize equipment, material and personnel;
- Site grading and retaining wall/bridge structure construction;
- BCS Stockpile Work Zone Control/Security;
- Worker and Equipment Decontamination;
- BCS Stockpile removal/consolidation within designated BCS Containment Zones;
- Management of placed BCS fill material;
- Dust and track-out control;
- Stormwater best management practices and monitoring;
- Placement of pavement and approved imported clean cover soil and vegetated ground cover over designated BCS Containment Zones; and
- As-built topographic survey data of completed BCS Containment Zones and clean cover soil/cap.

Geocon, under contract with the City, was responsible for verifying that the highway construction work associated with the soil stockpiles was performed in substantial conformance with the RAP and RDIP requirements. Geocon's responsibilities included:

- Observe and photo-document BCS excavation, fill placement and clean capping activities;
- Perform BCS removal verification soil sampling and analysis;
- Perform proposed clean soil import source sampling and analysis;
- Perform worker zone and perimeter air monitoring;
- Review RDIP requirements with the construction manager and highway construction contractor;
- Interact with and periodically update the Caltrans Environmental Manager and DTSC and CVRWQCB Project Managers;
- Provide status reports; and
- Prepare the RACR and Operation and Maintenance Plan (OMP).

#### 2.2 RDIP Preconstruction Meeting

Representatives of Caltrans, the City, DTSC, WSP, BCPG and Geocon attended a preconstruction project meeting on January 29, 2020 to review planned RDIP activities during SR 132 construction. Discussion items included communication control, notifications, schedule, time-lapse camera system update/access, health and safety, stormwater best management practices (BMPs) and monitoring, monitoring well

protection, decontamination protocols, BCS and ADL excavation/placement/containment, record keeping, dust mitigation, air monitoring, stop work authority, site preparation, topographic surveys before and after clean capping, and tarping of BCS/ADL transport trucks.

#### 3.0 RDIP ACTIVITIES AND BCS CONTAINMENT IMPLEMENTATION

This section summarizes the completed project activities outlined in the RDIP and the current status of consolidation, containment and clean fill capping of BCS Stockpiles 1 through 3 (and ADL contaminated soil) during Interim Phase 1 construction of the SR 132 West Project. Photographs of representative RDIP activities and BCS Stockpile grading activities are attached.

A representative of Geocon was onsite fulltime during the majority of the BCS Stockpile site preparation, excavation and fill placement activities, and periodically during BCS Containment Zone clean fill capping. Air monitoring for potential fugitive dust emissions was further implemented during the site preparation, BCS Stockpile excavation and fill placement activities as discussed in Section 3.5.

Excavated BCS was transported to the designated Stockpile 1 and 2 BCS Containment Zones for direct placement/compaction as permanent highway embankment fill. Construction geotechnical inspectors confirmed proper BCS, ADL and clean imported fill compaction through observation and in-place density testing.

#### 3.1 Groundwater Monitoring Well Abandonment/Protection

Groundwater monitoring wells associated with the Caltrans Modesto Soil Stockpiles (Figure 2) have been sampled on an annual basis typically during the late winter/early spring in accordance with regulatory directive. Six of the ten monitoring well locations required proper abandonment due to conflicts with the highway construction including retaining wall and drainage improvements. Monitoring wells MW1, MW2, MW3, MW5, MW7, and MW8 were destroyed in April 2019 under City of Modesto permit (Geocon, 2019). The wells were destroyed by pressure grouting and drilling out the upper 5 feet of the well casing.

The remaining four groundwater monitoring wells (MW4, MW6, MW9 and MW10) have been protected in place by the highway construction contractor and made available to Caltrans for sampling events during highway construction.

#### 3.2 Permitting/Utility Clearance

No regulatory permits were required to complete the containment and capping of the BCS Stockpiles since the work was completed under permits specifically obtained for construction of Interim Phase 1 of the SR 132 West Project. The highway construction contractor was responsible for obtaining any required permits prior to commencing with BCS grading/earthmoving activities. No active utilities were identified within the BCS Stockpile removal areas and designated BCS Containment Zones prior to the highway construction activities.

# 3.3 Time-lapse Photography

Time-lapse video photography was utilized to document the BCS Stockpile grading and clean capping operations and associated highway construction activities. Two professional grade time-lapse cameras provided by OxBlue were installed by the highway construction contractor to generate dated still images and high-definition time-lapse video. Camera #1 was installed on a utility pole at the southwest corner of the SR 132 ROW and Emerald Avenue. Camera #2 was installed on the roof top of a hotel located immediately northeast of SR 99 and SR 132 interchange. The digital images and video will remain the property of Caltrans.

#### 3.4 Site Preparation

A safety briefing was held the morning of February 18, 2021 with all field personnel working within the BCS Stockpile Exclusion Zones. The safety briefing provided by the BCPG Safety Manager consisted of a review of the project health and safety plan including site hazards, work practices, dust control, and decontamination procedures. Additional field personnel were required to attend a similar BCPG safety briefing prior to working within the BCS Stockpile Exclusion Zones.

BCS Stockpile 1 through 3 site preparation operations performed by BCPG included installation of perimeter fencing where required. The surface of Stockpiles 1 through 3 were mowed using weed whackers and power mowers. Following completed mowing operations, BCS Stockpile excavation and Containment Zone fill areas were disced/ripped, moisture-conditioned, and for BCS fill placement areas recompacted per project geotechnical specifications.

Decontamination stations for project personnel and construction vehicles/equipment were established near the east end of BCS Stockpile 1 on the west side of Emerald Avenue and at the south end of BCS Stockpile 3. Gravel track-out pads were installed at the gated construction vehicle entrance/exist points at both of these locations. Prior to existing the work zones the transport trucks and other constructionrelated vehicles/equipment were hand brushed to remove any residual soil.

#### 3.5 BCS Stockpile Grading Activities

The following sections present summaries of completed BCS Stockpile excavation, fill placement and clean fill capping operations

#### 3.5.1 Stockpile 3 BCS Removal/Placement

BCS Stockpile 3 was completely removed from the east side of SR 99 between February and March 2020. Excavated BCS material was loaded into transport trucks for placement as embankment fill within the designated BCS Stockpile 1 Containment Zone. The transport trucks were decontaminated and covered with tarps prior to leaving the Stockpile 3 Exclusion Zone. The removal of Stockpile 3 required excavation and transport of approximately 3,075 truckloads totaling approximately 27,675 yd<sup>3</sup> (based on 9 yd<sup>3</sup> per truck) of BCS material.

Twenty-nine verification soil samples were collected from 26 locations (S3-1 through S3-26) within the BCS Stockpile 3 removal area. Verification soil samples S3 1-0 through S3 24-0 were collected from native soil (except for S3 16-0) exposed at the base of the BCS Stockpile 3 excavation at approximate elevations ranging between 82.5 and 84 feet above mean sea level (MSL). Sample S3 16-0 was collected from storm drain trench backfill material. Verification soil samples S3 SE Berm and S3 SW Berm were collected from the top of the berm at the southern end of the Stockpile 3 excavation adjacent to the Modesto Irrigation District water canal.

Each verification soil sample was analyzed by Advanced Technology Laboratories (ATL) for barium and lead following Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) Test Method 6010B. Following receipt of the analytical data indicating elevated barium concentrations ranging from 2,200 to 7,000 mg/kg (above the verification threshold) for three sample locations, these excavation areas were deepened approximately one foot and resampled.

Each of the final verification soil samples contained barium and lead below the RDIP verification thresholds. Barium concentrations ranged from 46 to 970 mg/kg, and lead concentrations from 1.6 to 40 mg/kg. Based on the data, the Stockpile 3 BCS removal area met the barium and lead verification thresholds (1,000 and 80 mg/kg respectively) and was determined to be suitable for placement of clean embankment fill and other highway improvements without further BCS RDIP monitoring requirements.

The approximate BCS Stockpile 3 verification soil sample locations are depicted on Figure 3. A copy of the DTSC-approved BCS Stockpile 3 Removal Verification Testing Technical Memorandum dated March 13, 2020, including sample location plans, soil data summary table, and analytical laboratory reports, is in Appendix B.

#### 3.5.2 Stockpile 2 BCS Removal/Placement

The western and eastern ends of BCS Stockpile 2 were removed between February and March 2020 to facilitate clean construction of SR 132 bridge overcrossing abutments. The excavated BCS material was removed by scrapers for placement as embankment fill within the designated BCS Stockpile 2 Containment Zone. The western end of BCS Stockpile 2 (Emerald Avenue bridge abutment) was excavated to approximate Station 1135+35. The eastern end of BCS Stockpile 2 (SR 99 bridge abutment) was excavated to approximate Station 1148+30.

Ten verification soil samples were collected from eight locations (S2W-1 through S2W-8) at the excavated western end of BCS Stockpile 2. Fourteen verification soil samples were collected from twelve locations at the excavated eastern end of BCS Stockpile 2. Each verification sample was collected from native soil at approximate elevations ranging from 81 to 83 feet MSL.

Each verification soil sample was analyzed by ATL for barium and lead following EPA Test Method 6010B. Following receipt of the analytical data indicating elevated barium concentrations ranging from 1,100 to 4,100 mg/kg (above the verification threshold) for four sample locations, these excavation areas were deepened approximately one foot and resampled. Each of the final verification soil samples contained barium and lead below the RDIP verification thresholds. Barium concentrations ranged from 54 to 760 mg/kg, and lead concentrations from 1.5 to 35 mg/kg. Based on the data, the Stockpile 2 BCS western and eastern removal areas met the barium and lead verification thresholds (1,000 and 80 mg/kg respectively) and were determined to be suitable for placement of clean embankment fill and bridge abutment improvements without further BCS RDIP monitoring requirements.

The approximate western and eastern BCS Stockpile 2 verification soil sample locations are depicted on Figures 4a and 4b. A copy of the DTSC-approved BCS Stockpile 2 Removal Verification Testing Technical Memorandum dated March 9, 2020, including sample location plans, soil data summary table, and analytical laboratory reports, is in Appendix B.

#### 3.5.3 BCS Stockpiles 1 and 2 MSE Wall Soil Removal/Placement

The southerly slopes of BCS Stockpiles 1 and 2 were excavated (steepened) in March and April 2020 to facilitate clean construction of the MSE walls and to facilitate placement of specified wall reinforcement backfill materials. The excavated BCS slope material was removed by scrapers for placement as embankment fill within the designated BCS Stockpile 1 and 2 Containment Zones.

Soil within the MSE wall footing areas was sampled at one-foot intervals to the maximum footing depth of 3 to 5 feet to determine if the excavated soil was suitable for clean capping material. The sampling locations were spaced at approximate 200-foot intervals. Each soil sample was analyzed by ATL for barium and lead following EPA Test Method 6010B.

Elevated barium concentrations greater than the maximum site-specific background concentration of 120 mg/kg, but less than the RDIP removal threshold of 1,000 mg/kg, were detected in soil samples generally to a depth of 3 feet. Lead concentrations were generally within range of the site-specific background concentration of 3.8 mg/kg, and less than the RDIP removal threshold of 80 mg/kg. Based on the elevated barium concentrations, all of the excavated MSE wall footing material was placed in the BCS Stockpile 1 and 2 Containment Zones.

Copies of the Stockpiles 1 and 2 MSE Wall Footing Excavation Characterization Testing Technical Memorandums dated April 6 and June 18, 2020, including sample location plans, soil data summary tables, and analytical laboratory reports, are in Appendix C.

# 3.5.4 SR 99 ADL Soil Removal/Placement

ADL-contaminated soil excavated from the northbound unpaved shoulder area of SR 99 between L Street and the Kansas Avenue off-ramp was placed in the eastern end of the BCS Stockpile 1 Containment Zone in June and July of 2020. The ADL soil contains lead concentrations in excess of California and Federal hazardous waste criteria and was placed in accordance with the 2016 Soil Management Agreement for Aerially Deposited Lead-Contaminated Soils between DTSC and Caltrans (DTSC, 2016). "ADL-contaminated soil" is defined in the DTSC Agreement as excavated soil, based on a 95% upper confidence limit, that contains total lead greater than 80 mg/kg and/or soluble lead (Waste Extraction Test) greater than or equal to 5.0 milligrams per liter. ADL-contaminated soil reused under the DTSC Agreement must always be at least five feet above the highest groundwater elevation and, depending on lead concentrations, may need to be covered with at least one foot of clean soil or a pavement structure. ADL-contaminated soil may not be placed in areas where it might contact groundwater or surface water (such as streams and rivers), and must be buried in locations that are protected from erosion that may result from stormwater runoff.

#### 3.5.5 Utility Installation Within BCS Containment Zones

Trenching activities were performed within the BCS Stockpile 1 and 2 Containment Zones for the installation of storm drain pipe and \_\_\_ (pending as-built plan review). The excavated BCS material was utilized for trench backfill prior to placement of clean cover fill materials.

#### 3.5.6 Clean Fill Cap Material Source Evaluations and Placement

The following clean fill source areas identified by the highway construction contractor were sampled by Geocon between February 2020 and April 2021 for laboratory analysis to determine suitability for capping material in general accordance with DTSC's Information Advisory Clean Imported Fill Material (DTSC, 2001):

- SR 132 ROW construction excavation area located adjacent and east of Carpenter Road;
- Basin 5 construction excavation area adjacent and east of Carpenter Road;
- Bent 2 construction excavation stockpiles located adjacent and west of SR 99; and
- Carpenter Road shoofly construction excavation.

With the exception of the Bent 2 excavation stockpiles, each of the identified clean fill source areas was approved by DTSC for use as clean capping material over the BCS Stockpile 1 and 2 Containment Zones. Copies of the clean fill source area technical memos including sample location plans, soil data summary tables, and analytical laboratory reports are in Appendix D.

The majority of the upper surface and northern slopes of the completed Stockpile 1 and 2 BCS Containment Zones were capped with DTSC-approved clean fill material by the Fall 2020. The southern slopes of the completed Stockpile 1 and 2 BCS Containment Zones were capped with embankment fill and MSE wall backfill materials. The eastern and western ends of the Stockpile 1 and 2 BCS Containment Zones were filled against with embankment fill prior to construction of SR 99 and Emerald Road bridge structures.

In January 2021, a record rain event caused significant erosion of the clean cap fill materials placed on the exposed (unvegetated) northerly slopes of Stockpiles 1 and 2. Plastic sheeting was placed along the slopes to minimize further erosion pending a revised plan to stabilize the slope face. The plastic sheeting was removed and the eroded areas regraded in September 2021 using clean fill deposited at the toe of slope and placement of approved clean fill material generated from excavation of the Carpenter Road shoofly.

The cleaned capped northerly slopes of Stockpiles 1 and 2 were vegetated in October 2021 using a native plant mix hydroseed/hydromulch, and subsequent placement of an erosion control blanket (rolled erosion control product) and fiber rolls. The slope vegetation (and associated erosion control and drainage features) will be maintained by Caltrans until the Ultimate Phase 2 portion of the project completes final containment of BCS (i.e. retaining wall and highway pavement) along the northerly portions of Stockpiles 1 and 2.

#### 3.5.7 BCS Stockpile Containment Zone Survey

The highway construction contractor provided survey data of the final Stockpile 1 and 2 BCS Containment Zones prior to after placement of clean fill cover materials. A graphical depiction of the completed BCS Stockpile 1 and 2 Containment Zones generated by Dokken Engineering using the survey data is on Figure 5. A typical cross-section of the completed BCS Stockpile 1 and 2 Containment Zones prepared by Dokken Engineering is on Figure 6.

A boundary survey of the perimeter of the capped BCS Stockpile 1 and 2 Containment Zones was completed by Dokken Engineering. The boundary survey data will be utilized by Caltrans' for recording a regulatory-required land use covenant (LUC)/deed restriction with the Stanislaus County Clerk-Recorder Office. Copies of the survey data are in Appendix E.

# 4.0 RDIP MONITORING

This section presents a summary of completed groundwater/stormwater sampling, and air monitoring activities associated with BCS removal and placement activities during Interim Phase I construction of the SR 132 West Project.

#### 4.1 Groundwater/Stormwater Monitoring

Caltrans' subconsultant (Stantec) completed groundwater and stormwater monitoring activities in the vicinity of the BCS soil stockpiles during the construction of Interim Phase I of SR 132 West Project. Copies of the monitoring reports are accessible from the following regulatory EnviroStor and GeoTracker websites:

https://www.envirostor.dtsc.ca.gov/public/profile\_report.asp?global\_id=60001626

https://geotracker.waterboards.ca.gov/profile\_report.asp?global\_id=SL0609924194

Four groundwater monitoring events were completed for the remaining wells (MW-4, MW-6, MW-9 and MW-10) between November 2019 and March 2021. The November 2019 and August 2020 events included depth to water measurements only. The February 2020 and March 2021 events included depth to water measurements, groundwater sample collection and analytical testing. Reported concentrations of the target analytes for the two groundwater sampling events were below regulatory thresholds consistent with historical data. Annual groundwater sampling events will continue until it is determined by the CVRWQCB that the clean-capped BCS material does not pose a material threat to groundwater quality. Once this determination is made the remaining four monitoring wells may be properly destroyed.

Three stormwater sampling events specific to the BCS stockpile areas were completed by Stantec between December 2019 and December 2020. The monitoring events included five sampling locations consistent with previous sampling events along the perimeters of the BCS stockpiles and two background locations. The water sample analytical results were consistent with historical data. Stormwater originating from the BCS stockpiles and completed BCS Containment Zones was not observed to be migrating offsite. Additional stormwater sampling events will be performed in the vicinity of the BCS Stockpile 1 and 2 Containment Zones at regulatory-approved modified locations during qualifying rain events until completed construction of Ultimate Phase 2 of the SR 132 West Project..

#### 4.2 Air Monitoring

Air monitoring was performed between February 2020 and June 2020 during the following BCS grading activities:

- BCS stockpile mowing operations;
- Relocation of Stockpile 3 BCS material to Stockpile 2;

- Bridge abutment BCS excavations at east and west ends of Stockpile 2;
- Retaining wall BCS excavations along the southern slopes of Stockpiles 1 and 2;
- Stockpiles 1 and 2 northerly slope grading;
- Placement of BCS material within Stockpiles 1 and 2 fill containment zones; and
- Storm drain and other utility trenching within BCS Stockpiles 1 and 2.

Perimeter air monitoring was performed during the highway grading activities that involved significant disturbance of BCS stockpile material (surface preparation, excavation, loading, transport, fill placement, etc.). We provided ambient perimeter air monitoring during the BCS stockpile grading activities to document total airborne particulate and metals (i.e., barium and lead) concentrations in accordance with protocols presented in the RDIP. Results of the air monitoring determined that the contractor's dust control measures were effective in reducing airborne particulates below regulatory health-based thresholds. Air monitoring tasks included:

- Documenting and photographing the locations of air monitoring stations;
- Monitoring daily meteorological forecasts to anticipate onsite wind direction and speed;
- Collection of personal, upwind and downwind air samples over a minimum sampling period of 4.5 hours for analysis of barium, lead and total dust; and
- Verifying that downwind direct-read, real-time particulate counter readings (pDR-1200 monitors) did not exceed the preset Fence Line Action Level for total dust of 4.0 milligrams per cubic meter (mg/m<sup>3</sup> ).

Real-time MIE pDR-1200 direct reading instrument measurements were compared to the Fence Line Action Level of 4.0 mg/m<sup>3</sup> to confirm that "wet methods" used during BCS stockpile grading activities were effective in controlling offsite migration of total airborne dust. Real-time monitors were calibrated according to the manufacturer's instructions and programmed to log total dust levels every 60 seconds. In addition, audible alarms on monitors located downwind of excavation, soil handling, and sampling activities was set to the highest "Total Dust" level recorded on ambient upwind monitors.

Personal monitoring conducted during the BCS grading activities (required by project specifications) included representative sampling of equipment operators (open cab mower, compactor operator, etc.). Perimeter monitoring conducted during the BCS grading activities included one representative upwind (background) sample and at least two representative downwind (offsite) sampling locations. Air monitoring was performed using real-time direct reading particulate counters with PM-10 inlets and active sampling attachments for 37-millimeter (mm) filter cassettes using personal sampling pumps calibrated at 2.0 to 3.0 liters per minute. Match-weighed 0.8-micron mixed cellulose ester filters were analyzed under expedited turn-around-times by LA Testing for total dust following National Institute for Occupational Safety and Health (NIOSH) 0500 and barium and lead in accordance with NIOSH 7300 modified.

Field direct-read and laboratory total dust data was compared to the RDIP Fence Line Action Level of 4.0 mg/m³. Barium results were compared to an action level of 25 micrograms per cubic meter (μg/ m³) as presented in the highway contractors' *Health & Safety Plan* prepared by Adanta, Inc., November 2019 (Adanta, 2019). Lead results were compared to an action level of 1.5 ug/μm³ as presented by the California Air Resources Board 30-day average ambient lead public health standard.

The results of air monitoring performed during BCS grading activities for the Project indicate airborne levels of total dust, barium, and lead below designated project action and risk levels, confirming effective dust control measures. A copy of the BCS Air Monitoring Summary Technical Memorandum dated September 2020 is in Appendix F.

#### 4.3 Ultimate Phase 2 Supplemental RDIP

Prior to construction of Ultimate Phase 2 of the SR 132 West Project, a supplemental RDIP will be submitted to DTSC for approval. The Supplemental RDIP will provide monitoring and sampling requirements for any planned BCS disturbance, groundwater monitoring well abandonment/protective measures if applicable, and copies of final grading/improvement design plans. A Final RACR will be submitted following completed construction of Ultimate Phase 2 of the SR 132 West Project.

# 5.0 POST CONSTRUCTION MONITORING/MAINTENANCE

This section describes monitoring and maintenance activities that will be conducted subsequent to BCS Stockpile containment and capping measures implemented during Interim Phase 1 of the SR 132 West Project that have been completed in substantial conformance with the RDIP.

#### 5.1 Operation and Maintenance

Caltrans will enter into an Operation and Maintenance Agreement with DTSC for implementation and oversight of the Operation and Maintenance Plan (OMP) presented in Appendix G. The OMP requires periodic inspection of the installed containment measures (travel lane pavement, hardscape and vegetated clean fill cover) to confirm the measures are properly maintained, intact and properly functioning as exposure barriers to the environment and direct contact. The inspections will also confirm that perimeter walls and fencing are effective in restricting access to authorized personnel only. The identification of, and implementation schedule for, any required corrective action measures will be included in annual inspection reports submitted to DTSC.

#### 5.2 Land Use Covenant

A recorded LUC for the contained and capped soil stockpiles, including the BCS Containment Zone boundary survey, will be prepared by DTSC to restrict the types of land use and future activities that are allowed on the Site. The LUC will recognize that the proposed transportation land use is compatible and is acceptable from a health risk perspective. Unrestricted land uses (e.g., residential, schools, daycare, hospital, senior care, etc.) will not be allowed on the Site. Notification to DTSC will be required for any future BCS Containment Zone disturbance activities (i.e. slope repair, utility maintenance, etc.) to ensure proper soil management and worker protection measures.

The LUC will be prepared consistent with DTSC policy and finalized and recorded with the Stanislaus County Clerk-Recorder Office before the Site is certified by the DTSC as having been remediated. The LUC (including geospatial data that defines the Stockpiles 1 and 2 BCS Containment Zones) will run with the land and stay in effect as long as hazardous substances limit use of the Site and until terminated by the DTSC. Pursuant to California Code of Regulations Section 67391.1 of Title 22, Division 4.5, Chapter 39, Caltrans as the responsible party will pay all costs for DTSC oversight associated with administration of the LUC. The DTSC has authority to require modification or removal of any land improvements placed in violation of the restrictions. Violation of the LUC will be grounds for DTSC to file civil or criminal actions as provided by law.

# 5.3 Five-Year Review

Caltrans will perform five-year reviews to assess the effectiveness of the interim and final BCS containment measures. The five-year reviews will focus on monitoring and maintenance of the implemented containment measures and potential for exposure to contained and capped BCS/ADL soil materials. Monitoring of groundwater and stormwater are routinely reported to DTSC and the CVRWQCB until permanently suspended and therefore would not be a focus of the reviews. DTSC will provide project certification following completion of the final containment measures implemented during Ultimate Phase 2 of the SR 132 West Project.

# APPENDIX A

#### GEOTECHNICAL . ENVIRONMENTAL . MATERIALS

![](_page_45_Picture_2.jpeg)

Project No. S1908-01-01 April 9, 2020

Mr. Dean Wright California Department of Toxic Substances Control 8800 Cal Center Drive Sacramento, California 95826

Subject: VARIANCE TO REMEDIAL DESIGN IMPLEMENTATION PLAN

CALTRANS MODESTO SOIL STOCKPILES

INTERIM PHASE 1 STATE ROUTE 132 WEST PROJECT MODESTO, STANISLAUS COUNTY, CALIFORNIA

Reference Document: Remedial Design Implementation Plan - Caltrans Modesto Soil Stockpiles,

Interim Phase I State Route 132 West Project, Modesto, Stanislaus County,

California, January 30, 2019.

Mr. Wright:

In accordance with your request on behalf of the California Department of Toxic Substances Control (DTSC), we have prepared this Variance to Remedial Design Implementation Plan (RDIP Variance) for the Caltrans Modesto Soil Stockpiles located south of the State Route (SR) 99/Kansas Avenue interchange in Modesto, Stanislaus County, California. This RDIP Variance has been prepared to address the identified sources of clean cover fill materials to properly cap consolidated barium-containing soil (BCS) within Stockpiles 1 and 2 during the SR 132 West Project.

Section 4.7 of the RDIP contains the following text with respect to evaluation and approval of identified sources for clean cover fill materials:

"The surface of the completed Stockpile 1 and 2 barium-containing soil and ADL consolidation zones (including east- and west-facing temporary slopes) will be capped with minimum 12-inch-thick clean fill material consisting of approved imported fill and/or minimum 4-inch-thick AB materials (pending placement of structural pavement section, hardscape and/or additional clean embankment fill). The proposed source of clean imported fill soil consists of currently vacant and agricultural land within planned excavation areas along the SR-132 ROW between the Carpenter Road and Rosemore Avenue overcrossings as depicted on Figure 1. The specific clean fill source areas (once defined by the highway construction contractor) will be sampled by Geocon for laboratory analysis in general accordance with DTSC's *Information Advisory Clean Imported Fill Material* (Appendix E) for pre-approval by DTSC prior to placement."

The highway contractor (Bay Cities Paving and Grading) initially identified an area adjacent and east of Carpenter Road within the SR 132 right-of-way as the clean fill material source for capping Stockpiles 1 and 2. Geocon, in coordination with Caltrans and DTSC, evaluated this area and determined the planned highway underpass excavation area adjacent and east of Carpenter Road would generate soil material suitable for use as clean cover fill material (Geocon February 27, 2020 Technical Memorandum). DTSC subsequently provided approval of the planned Carpenter Road clean fill source area.

An additional source of clean fill material was identified by the highway contractor at the proposed mechanically stabilized earth (MSE) wall footing excavations (approximately five-foot-deep) located along the southern boundaries of Stockpiles 1 and 2. Geocon evaluated the MSE wall footing material along the southern boundary of Stockpile 1 following excavation of the southern slope BCS material (Geocon April 6, 2020 Technical Memorandum). In coordination with Caltrans and DTSC, it was determined that the upper foot of planned wall footing excavation soil contained elevated barium concentrations that would not be suitable for use as clean cover fill material (to be placed in Stockpile 1 and 2 permanent fill areas). Soil extending from 1 to 5 feet from existing grade contained barium concentrations ranging from 42 to 140 milligrams per kilogram (mg/kg) with a calculated 95% upper confidence limit (UCL) of 113.1 mg/kg, within the range of site-specific naturally occurring background barium concentrations ranging from 17 to 120 mg/kg (see attached data summary, statistical output and site-specific background summary).

The MSE wall footing excavation located along the southern boundary of Stockpile 2 (approximately five-foot-deep) will be evaluated once the southern slope BCS material is excavated. Soil samples will be analyzed for barium and lead and compared to site-specific naturally occurring background concentrations to determine if suitable for use as clean cover fill material.

It is understood that the highway contractor intends to use the approved Carpenter Road borrow source area and any DTSC-approved wall footing excavation material for use as clean cover material on Stockpiles 1 and 2. No other clean fill material sources are proposed.

Please contact the undersigned should you have any questions regarding this RDIP Variance.

Sincerely,

GEOCON CONSULTANTS, INC.

John E. Juhrend, PE, CEG

Attachments: Stockpile 1 MSE Wall Data Summary, 95% UCL Statistical Output, and Site-

#### GEOTECHNICAL . ENVIRONMENTAL . MATERIALS

![](_page_52_Picture_2.jpeg)

Project No. S1908-01-01 September 2, 2020

Mr. Dean Wright California Department of Toxic Substances Control 8800 Cal Center Drive Sacramento, California 95826

Subject: SECOND VARIANCE TO REMEDIAL DESIGN IMPLEMENTATION PLAN

CALTRANS MODESTO SOIL STOCKPILES

INTERIM PHASE 1 STATE ROUTE 132 WEST PROJECT MODESTO, STANISLAUS COUNTY, CALIFORNIA

Reference Document: Remedial Design Implementation Plan - Caltrans Modesto Soil Stockpiles,

Interim Phase I State Route 132 West Project, Modesto, Stanislaus County,

California, January 30, 2019.

Mr. Wright:

In accordance with your request on behalf of the California Department of Toxic Substances Control (DTSC), we have prepared this Second Variance to Remedial Design Implementation Plan (RDIP Variance #2) for the Caltrans Modesto Soil Stockpiles located south of the State Route (SR) 99/Kansas Avenue interchange in Modesto, Stanislaus County, California. This RDIP Variance #2 has been prepared to address clean cover fill material proposed by the highway contractor to properly cap the upper surface of consolidated barium-containing soil (BCS) within Stockpiles 1 and 2 during the SR 132 West Project.

Section 3.2.2 of the RDIP contains the following text with respect to BCS stockpile clean cover fill materials:

"Placement of minimum 12-inch-thick clean cover fill material over the barium-containing soil and ADL consolidation zones. As an alternative, minimum 4-inch-thick aggregate base (AB) materials may be placed on the upper stockpile surface pending subsequent placement of structural pavement and hardscape."

The intent of the RDIP option for capping the upper surface of the completed BCS and aerially-deposited lead (ADL) fill containment zones of Stockpiles 1 and 2 with 4 inches of aggregate base materials was to preserve fill capacity with the understanding that the structural pavement section and hardscape would provide the ultimate clean cap for the upper surface of the stockpiles. The specified structural pavement section for the Interim Phase I of the SR 132 West Project consists of the following:

0.85 feet (10.2 inches) concrete pavement 0.25 feet (3.0 inches) hot mix asphalt 0.70 feet (8.4 inches) Class 3 aggregate subbase

Areas outside of the highway structural pavement section on the upper surface of the stockpiles will be capped with hardscape.

The following presents Caltrans' gradation summaries for aggregate subbase materials:

#### 25-1.02 MATERIALS

#### 25-1.02A General

Aggregate for Class 1, 2, 3, and 4 AS must be clean and consist of any combination of the following:

- 1 Broken stone
- 2. Crushed gravel
- 3. Natural rough surfaced gravel
- 4. Sand
- Reclaimed processed asphalt concrete, PCC, LCB, or CTB

If Class 5 is specified, the location for obtaining the material and gradation requirements are in the special provisions.

#### 25-1.02B Class 1, Class 2, and Class 3 Aggregate Subbases

Aggregate gradation must be within the percentage passing limits for the sieve sizes shown in the following table:

#### Aggregate Gradation

| Sieve size | Class 1         |                     | Class 2         |                     | Class 3         |                     |
|------------|-----------------|---------------------|-----------------|---------------------|-----------------|---------------------|
|            | Operating range | Contract compliance | Operating range | Contract compliance | Operating range | Contract compliance |
| 3"         | 100             | 100                 | 100             | 100                 | 100             | 100                 |
| 2 1/2"     | 90-100          | 87-100              | 90-100          | 87-100              | 90-100          | 87-100              |
| No. 4      | 35-70           | 30-75               | 40-90           | 35-95               | 50-100          | 45-100              |
| No. 200    | 0-20            | 0-23                | 0-25            | 0-29                | 0-30            | 0-34                |

The aggregate quality characteristics must comply with the requirements for the classes shown in the following table:

#### Aggregate Quality Characteristics

| Quality characteristic     | Class 1         |                     | Class 2         |                     | Class 3         |                     |
|----------------------------|-----------------|---------------------|-----------------|---------------------|-----------------|---------------------|
|                            | Operating range | Contract compliance | Operating range | Contract compliance | Operating range | Contract compliance |
| Sand equivalent, (min)     | 21              | 18                  | 21              | 18                  | 21              | 18                  |
| Resistance, (R-value, min) | --              | 60                  | --              | 50                  | --              | 40                  |

Review of the gradation summaries indicates that the aggregate subbase materials are primarily comprised of sand and gravel with lesser fines content similar to aggregate base materials. The planned placement of 4 inches of Class 3 aggregate subbase materials on the upper surface of Stockpiles 1 and 2 would provide adequate interim clean cover (equivalent to aggregate base for purposes of cover material) until the remainder of the pavement section/hardscape is placed.

Based on the information presented herein, the use of a minimum of 4 inches of Class 3 aggregate subbase materials as clean cover fill on the upper surface of Stockpiles 1 and 2 meets the intent of the RDIP and would provide adequate clean cover until the remaining pavement section/hardscape is placed.

Please contact the undersigned should you have any questions regarding this RDIP Variance #2.

Sincerely,

GEOCON CONSULTANTS, INC.

John E. Juhrend, PE, CEG

DTSC, Mr. Perry Myers

# APPENDIX B

![](_page_56_Picture_0.jpeg)

#### Technical Memorandum

Date: March 13, 2020

To: Rick Demi, PE, Construction Manager

WSP/City of Modesto

From: John E. Juhrend, PE, CEG, Project Manager, Geocon

Subject: Stockpile 3 BCS Removal Verification Testing

State Route 132 Project, Modesto, California

This Technical Memorandum presents a summary of barium containing soil (BCS) removal verification testing completed at former Stockpile 3 within the State Route 132 right-of-way (see attached plan sheet). Stockpile 3 BCS material located adjacent and east of State Route 99 between Kansas Avenue and the Modesto Irrigation District (MID) water canal was excavated and transported for placement as embankment fill within the designated Stockpile 1 fill containment zone. A total of 3,075 truck-loads of BCS material totaling approximately 38,440 cubic yards (preliminary estimate based on 12.5 cubic yards per truck) was transported to Stockpile 1.

The BCS removal verification testing was performed in general accordance with the January 2019 Remedial Design Implementation Plan (RDIP) prepared for the State Route 132 Project. The RDIP provides the following verification testing thresholds, and Stockpile 3 excavation target elevation and minimum number of verification soil samples:

#### **BCS Removal Verification Testing Removal Thresholds**

Barium - 1,000 milligrams per kilogram (mg/kg, groundwater protection) Lead - 80 mg/kg (residential screening level)

| Excavation Area | Original Ground Surface Elevation<br>(Above Mean Sea Level<br>[MSL]) | Minimum Number of Verification Samples |
|-----------------|----------------------------------------------------------------------|----------------------------------------|
| Stockpile 3     | 84                                                                   | 24                                     |

Stockpile 3 was excavated to approximate elevations ranging between 82.5 and 84 feet above MSL. A total of 24 verification soil samples (S3 1-0 through S3 24-0) were collected between February 28 and March 12, 2020 from the base of the excavation. Following receipt of the analytical data indicating elevated barium concentrations above the verification threshold for sample locations S3 9-0 through S3 11-0, these excavation areas were deepened approximately one foot and resampled. Verification soil samples S3 SE BERM and S3 SW BERM were collected from the top of the berm at the southern end of the Stockpile 3 excavation adjacent to the MID water canal.

![](_page_56_Picture_14.jpeg)

The BCS removal verification soil samples were collected from the base of the removal areas that exposed native soil conditions with the exception of sample location S3 16-0 that was collected from storm drain backfill materials adjacent to State Route 99. The verification soil samples were collected using new stainless-steel sample tubes that were transported to Advanced Technology Laboratories (ATL) under chain of custody protocol. Each soil sample was analyzed for barium and lead following Environmental Protection Agency Test Method 6010B. The approximate verification soil sample locations are depicted on the attached plan sheet. A summary of the verification soil sample analytical data is on Table 1. Copies of the ATL laboratory reports are attached.

Each of the final excavation soil samples contained barium and lead below the RDIP verification thresholds. Barium concentrations ranged from 46 to 970 mg/kg, and lead concentrations from 1.6 to 40 mg/kg. Based on the data presented herein, the Stockpile 3 BCS removal has met the barium and lead verification thresholds and is suitable for placement of clean embankment fill and other highway improvements without further BCS RDIP monitoring requirements. This information will be included in the Removal Action Completion Report to be submitted following completion of the Stockpile 1 and 2 clean fill capping activities.

![](_page_58_Figure_0.jpeg)

#### **Notes and Definitions**

M2 Matrix spike recovery outside of acceptance limit due to possible matrix interference. The analytical batch was validated by the laboratory

control sample.

ND Analyte is not detected at or above the Practical Quantitation Limit (PQL). When client requests quantitation against MDL,

analyte is not detected at or above the Method Detection Limit (MDL)

PQL Practical Quantitation Limit

MDL Method Detection Limit

NR Not Reported

RPD Relative Percent Difference

CA2 CA-ELAP (CDPH)

OR1 OR-NELAP (OSPHL)

#### Notes:

- (1) The reported MDL and PQL are based on prep ratio variation and analytical dilution.
- (2) The suffix [2C] of specific analytes signifies that the reported result is taken from the instrument's second column.
- (3) Results are wet unless otherwise specified.

#### **Notes and Definitions**

ND Analyte is not detected at or above the Practical Quantitation Limit (PQL). When client requests quantitation against MDL,

analyte is not detected at or above the Method Detection Limit (MDL)

(1) The reported MDL and PQL are based on prep ratio variation and analytical dilution.

(2) The suffix [2C] of specific analytes signifies that the reported result is taken from the instrument's second column.

(3) Results are wet unless otherwise specified.

#### **Notes and Definitions**

M2 Matrix spike recovery outside of acceptance limit due to possible matrix interference. The analytical batch was validated by the laboratory

control sample.

ND Analyte is not detected at or above the Practical Quantitation Limit (PQL). When client requests quantitation against MDL,

analyte is not detected at or above the Method Detection Limit (MDL)

PQL Practical Quantitation Limit

MDL Method Detection Limit

NR Not Reported

RPD Relative Percent Difference

CA2 CA-ELAP (CDPH)

OR1 OR-NELAP (OSPHL)

#### Notes:

(1) The reported MDL and PQL are based on prep ratio variation and analytical dilution.

(2) The suffix [2C] of specific analytes signifies that the reported result is taken from the instrument's second column.

(3) Results are wet unless otherwise specified.

#### Technical Memorandum

Date: March 9, 2020

To: Rick Demi, PE, Construction Manager

WSP/City of Modesto

From: John E. Juhrend, PE, CEG, Project Manager, Geocon

Subject: Stockpile 2 BCS Removal Verification Testing

State Route 132 Project, Modesto, California

This Technical Memorandum presents a summary of barium containing soil (BCS) removal verification testing completed at the west and east ends of Stockpile 2 within the State Route 132 right-of-way (see attached plan sheets). The BCS excavation areas include the west end of Stockpile 2 adjacent to Emerald Avenue and the east end of Stockpile 2 adjacent to State Route 99. BCS was removed from these areas to facilitate construction of bridge overcrossing structures. The removed BCS was placed as embankment fill within the designated Stockpile 2 fill containment zones.

The BCS removal verification testing was performed in general accordance with the January 2019 Remedial Design Implementation Plan (RDIP) prepared for the State Route 132 Project. The RDIP provides the following verification testing thresholds, excavation elevations and minimum number of verification soil samples:

#### **BCS Removal Verification Testing Removal Thresholds**

Barium - 1,000 milligrams per kilogram (mg/kg, groundwater protection) Lead - 80 mg/kg (residential screening level)

| Excavation Area      | Original Ground Surface Elevation (Above Mean Sea Level [MSL]) | Minimum Number of Verification Samples |
|----------------------|----------------------------------------------------------------|----------------------------------------|
| West End Stockpile 2 | 80                                                             | 8                                      |
| East End Stockpile 2 | 82                                                             | 12                                     |

The west end of Stockpile 2 was excavated to an approximate elevation of 82 feet above MSL. Eight soil samples (S2W 1-0 through 8-0) were collected on February 28, 2020 from the base of the excavation. Following receipt of the initial analytical data indicating elevated barium concentrations above the verification threshold for sample locations S2W 1-0 and S2W 3-0, the western half of the excavation was deepened to an approximate elevation of 81 feet above MSL. Two additional verification soil samples were collected from the deepened excavation on March 6, 2020.

The eastern half of the east end of Stockpile 2 was excavated to an approximate elevation of 83 feet above MSL. Six soil samples (S2E 1-0 through 8-0) were collected on February 21, 2020 from the

![](_page_115_Picture_15.jpeg)

base of the excavation. Following receipt of the initial analytical data indicating an elevated barium concentration above the verification threshold for sample location S2E 3-0, the area around this sample location and the western half of the excavation was excavated to an approximate elevation of 82 feet above MSL. Seven additional verification soil samples (S2E 3A-0 and S2E 7-0 through 12-0) were collected from the western half of the excavation on February 28, 2020. Only sample location SE2 8-0 contained an elevated barium concentration above the verification threshold. This area was subsequently excavated to an approximate elevation of 81 feet above MSL. One additional verification soil sample was collected from the deepened excavation on March 6, 2020.

The BCS removal verification soil samples were collected from the base of the removal areas that exposed native soil conditions using new stainless steel sample tubes that were transported to Advanced Technology Laboratories (ATL) under chain of custody protocol. Each soil sample was analyzed for barium and lead following Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) Test Method 6010B. The approximate verification soil sample locations are depicted on the attached plan sheets. A summary of the verification soil sample analytical data is on Table 1. Copies of the ATL laboratory reports are attached.

Each of the final excavation soil samples contained barium and lead below the RDIP verification thresholds. Barium concentrations ranged from 54 to 760 mg/kg, and lead concentrations from 1.5 to 35 mg/kg. Based on the data presented herein, the BCS removal at the west and east ends of Stockpile 2 has met the barium and lead verification thresholds and is suitable for placement of clean embankment fill. This information will be included in the Removal Action Completion Report to be submitted following completion of the stockpile clean fill capping activities.

- 2. -

ATLCOC Ver: 20130715

For Laboratory Use Only

The following are the results of the experiment:

- **Experiment 1:**

  - **Objective:** To measure the effect of temperature on reaction rate.
  - **Procedure:**
    1. Prepare solutions of reactants.
    2. Vary the temperature of the reaction mixture.
    3. Measure the time taken for the reaction to complete.
  - **Results:**
    - At 25°C, the reaction took 10 minutes.
    - At 50°C, the reaction took 5 minutes.
    - At 75°C, the reaction took 2.5 minutes.
  - **Conclusion:** Reaction rate increases with temperature.
- **Experiment 2:**

  - **Objective:** To determine the effect of catalyst concentration on reaction rate.
  - **Procedure:**
    1. Prepare solutions of reactants.
    2. Vary the concentration of the catalyst.
    3. Measure the time taken for the reaction to complete.
  - **Results:**
    - 0.1M catalyst: 8 minutes
    - 0.2M catalyst: 4 minutes
    - 0.3M catalyst: 2 minutes
  - **Conclusion:** Reaction rate increases with catalyst concentration.

**Overall Conclusion:** Both temperature and catalyst concentration significantly affect reaction rate. Increasing either leads to a faster reaction.

| TAT = 0: 100% Surcharge SAME BUSINESS DAY if received by 9:00 AM.                                                                                                                                                                                                    |
| TAT = 1: 100% Surcharge NEXT BUSINESS DAY (COB 5:00 PM)                                                                                                                                                                                                              |
| TAT = 2: 50% Surcharge NEXT BUSINESS DAY (COB 5:00 PM)                                                                                                                                                                                                               |

| 5. Subcontract (TAT is 10 - 15 business days. Projects requiring shorter TATs will incur a surcharge respective to the subcontract lab - ask for quote.                                                                                                              |
| 6. Liquid and solid samples will be disposed of after 45 calendar days from receipt of samples; air samples will be disposed of after 14 calendar days after receipt of samples.                                                                                     |

| 8. Hard copy reports will be disposed of after 45 calendar days from report date.                                                                                                                                                                                    |

| Liquid & solid samples: Complimentary storage for forty-five (45) calendar days from receipt of samples; \$2/sample/month if extended storage or hold is requested.                                                                                                  |

#### **Notes and Definitions**

D6 Sample required dilution due to high concentration of target analyte.

ND Analyte is not detected at or above the Practical Quantitation Limit (PQL). When client requests quantitation against MDL,

analyte is not detected at or above the Method Detection Limit (MDL)

PQL Practical Quantitation Limit

MDL Method Detection Limit

NR Not Reported

RPD Relative Percent Difference

CA2 CA-ELAP (CDPH)
OR1 OR-NELAP (OSPHL)

#### Notes

- (1) The reported MDL and PQL are based on prep ratio variation and analytical dilution.
- (2) The suffix [2C] of specific analytes signifies that the reported result is taken from the instrument's second column.
- (3) Results are wet unless otherwise specified.

# APPENDIX C

![](_page_170_Picture_0.jpeg)

#### Technical Memorandum

Date: April 6, 2020

To: Rick Demi, PE, Construction Manager

WSP/City of Modesto

From: John E. Juhrend, PE, CEG, Project Manager, Geocon

Subject: Stockpile 1 MSE Wall Footing Excavation Characterization Testing

State Route 132 Project, Modesto, California

This Technical Memorandum presents a summary of mechanically stabilized earth (MSE) wall footing excavation characterization testing performed following completed removal of barium containing soil (BCS) along the Stockpile 1 southerly slope within the State Route 132 right-of-way (see attached plan sheets). Stockpile 1 southerly slope BCS material was excavated and transported for placement as embankment fill within the designated Stockpile 1 fill containment zone.

We collected soil samples within the mid-point of the wall footing excavation in trenches STK1-1, STK1-2 and STK1-3 spaced at approximate 200-foot intervals. Discrete soil samples were collected from one-foot intervals to the maximum planned wall footing excavation depth of 5 feet. The soil samples were transported to Advanced Technology Laboratories (ATL) under chain of custody protocol. Each soil sample was analyzed for barium and lead following Environmental Protection Agency Test Method 6010B under expediated 24-hour turn-around time. The approximate soil sample locations are depicted on the attached plan sheets. A summary of the soil sample analytical data is on Table 1. A copy of the ATL laboratory report is attached.

Barium was detected in each of the 15 soil samples analyzed at concentrations ranging from 42 to 420 milligrams per kilogram (mg/kg), greater than the maximum site specific background value of 120 mg/kg, but less than the Remedial Design Implementation Plan (RDIP) removal threshold of 1,000 mg/kg based on groundwater quality protection. The calculated 95% upper confidence limit (UCL) and mean of the entire barium data set are 171.9 and 128.3 mg/kg, respectively (statistical summary attached). The San Francisco Bay Regional Water Quality Control Board's Environmental Screening Levels (ESLs) for barium are 3,000 mg/kg for construction workers, 15,000 mg/kg for residential land use, and 220,000 mg/kg for commercial land use.

Lead was detected in 14 of 15 soil samples analyzed at concentrations ranging from 1.1 to 6.7 mg/kg, within the range of the maximum site-specific background concentration of 3.8 mg/kg, and less than the RDIP removal threshold of 80 mg/kg based on residential land use.

Based on the data presented herein, the Stockpile 1 MSE wall footing material excavated along the southerly boundary of Stockpile 1 should be suitable for placement within the Stockpiles 1 and 2 fill containment zones, as general bridge embankment fill at the east end of Stockpile 1 and/or ends of Stockpile 2, or as Stockpile 1 and 2 capping material pending regulatory agency approval. This information will be included in the Removal Action Completion Report to be submitted following completion of the Stockpile 1 and 2 clean fill capping activities.

#### Notes:

(1) The reported MDL and PQL are based on prep ratio variation and analytical dilution.

(2) The suffix [2C] of specific analytes signifies that the reported result is taken from the instrument's second column.

(3) Results are wet unless otherwise specified.

# **Technical Memorandum**

Date: June 18, 2020

To: Michael Scott, Construction Manager

WSP/City of Modesto

From: John E. Juhrend, PE, CEG, Project Manager, Geocon

Subject: Stockpile 2 MSE Wall Footing Excavation Characterization Testing

State Route 132 Project, Modesto, California

Reference: Variance to Remedial Design Implementation Plan, April 9, 2020

This Technical Memorandum presents a summary of mechanically stabilized earth (MSE) wall footing excavation soil characterization testing performed following completed removal of barium containing soil (BCS) along the Stockpile 2 southerly slope within the State Route 132 right-of-way (see attached plan sheets). Stockpile 2 southerly slope BCS material was excavated to facilitate construction of an MSE wall. The excavated BCS material was transported for placement as embankment fill within the designated Stockpile 2 fill containment zone.

We collected soil samples within the mid-point of the planned wall footing excavation from trenches STK2-1 through STK2-7 spaced at approximate 200-foot intervals. Discrete soil samples were collected from one-foot intervals to the maximum planned wall footing excavation depth of 3 and 5 feet. The soil samples were transported to Advanced Technology Laboratories (ATL) under chain of custody protocol. Each soil sample was analyzed for barium and lead following Environmental Protection Agency Test Method 6010B under expediated 48-hour turn-around time. The approximate soil sample locations are depicted on the attached plan sheets. A summary of the soil sample analytical data is on Table 1. A copy of the ATL laboratory report is attached.

Barium was detected in each of the 29 soil samples analyzed at concentrations ranging from 53 to 930 milligrams per kilogram (mg/kg). Fourteen of the 29 soil samples contained barium at concentrations greater than the maximum site-specific background value for barium of 120 mg/kg. Thirteen of the 14 soil samples with elevated barium concentrations above the maximum background value were collected at depths between the surface and 3 feet. Sample STK2-7-4 contained a barium concentration of 140 mg/kg, though this sample was collected deeper than the planned footing excavation depth of 3 feet at this location.

Lead was detected in each of the soil samples analyzed at concentrations ranging from 3.5 to 22 mg/kg. The majority of the soil samples contained reported lead concentrations within the same order of magnitude as the maximum site-specific background concentration for lead of 3.8 mg/kg.

Based on the data presented herein, the upper three feet of soil excavated from the Stockpile 2 southerly slope MSE wall footing will require placement within the designated Stockpiles 1 and 2 fill containment zones. Stockpile 2 southerly slope MSE wall footing material excavated from depths deeper than 3 feet is suitable for placement within the Stockpiles 1 and 2 fill containment zones, as general embankment fill, or as Stockpile 1 and 2 capping material pending regulatory agency approval. This information will be included in the Removal Action Completion Report to be submitted following completion of the Stockpile 1 and 2 clean fill capping activities.

#### **Notes and Definitions**

M2 Matrix spike recovery outside of acceptance limit due to possible matrix interference. The analytical batch was validated by the laboratory

control sample.

ND Analyte is not detected at or above the Practical Quantitation Limit (PQL). When client requests quantitation against MDL,

analyte is not detected at or above the Method Detection Limit (MDL)

PQL Practical Quantitation Limit

MDL Method Detection Limit

NR Not Reported

RPD Relative Percent Difference

CA2 CA-ELAP (CDPH)

OR1 OR-NELAP (OSPHL)

#### Notes:

- (1) The reported MDL and PQL are based on prep ratio variation and analytical dilution.
- (2) The suffix [2C] of specific analytes signifies that the reported result is taken from the instrument's second column.
- (3) Results are wet unless otherwise specified.
